output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.85, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Summary: Critique of the paragraph, flagging an unsupported empirical premise, an overreaching causal claim, prose problems (run-ons, tense slips), and missing citations; followed by a tightened rewrite.

**Substantive problems**

1. The "quarter of items → annotators anchor on the worst items → rating quality collapses" claim is asserted as a premise the reader must accept without argument. This is not a self-evident axiom; it is an empirical hypothesis about annotator behavior. Either cite anchoring/contrast-effect work in crowdsourced rating (e.g., Mathur et al. on Likert anchoring; work on assimilation/contrast in AMT batches) or demote the claim from premise to motivating hypothesis. As written, the entire pipeline rests on an unsupported number ("roughly a quarter") with no source.

2. The claim that "deep neural anomaly detectors uniformly outperform classical baselines … across the full range of anomaly-detection settings" is too strong and is uncited. The literature (e.g., Han et al., ADBench, NeurIPS 2022; Ruff et al.'s own survey) actually shows the opposite in several tabular and low-data regimes: classical methods such as iForest remain competitive or superior. Treating this as a "closed question" to justify skipping detector comparison is not defensible and will draw a reviewer objection. Either run the comparison, cite a domain-matched benchmark that supports the claim for text embeddings specifically, or soften to "prior work suggests deep detectors are competitive on high-dimensional embeddings, and we adopt Deep SVDD on that basis."

3. The causal attribution is overreaching: "we can attribute any downstream quality lift in the AMT ratings entirely to the gating step itself" does not follow from holding the embedding fixed. It follows only if you also control the generator, the HIT composition, the annotator pool, and the rating instrument across conditions. Fixing the encoder rules out one confound, not all of them. Rephrase as "isolates the contribution of the scoring head" rather than "entirely to the gating step."

4. "Generalizes across both generator architectures and target genres without per-deployment tuning" is asserted, not demonstrated. The training distribution is one source (WritingPrompts) in one genre (short creative fiction). Cross-genre and cross-generator generalization is an empirical question for the experiments section, not a property that follows "by virtue of its detector family."

5. Threshold choice: dropping everything above the 90th percentile of the *human* score distribution will drop ~10% of human stories by construction. State this explicitly and justify the choice, or calibrate on a held-out human set with a target FPR.

6. "Twelve thousand … from the WritingPrompts subreddit" needs a citation to the Fan et al. (2018) WritingPrompts dataset and a note on licensing/scraping provenance.

7. Tense and voice are inconsistent: "was then trained," "we will score," "are dropped" appear in the same sentence describing the same pipeline. Pick one (present tense is conventional for methods).

8. Both paragraphs are dominated by comma splices and run-on sentences strung together with "and." The second sentence of paragraph 1 is ~75 words across four independent clauses. Break these up.

9. "Storyguard" is introduced but never motivated by name; the name carries no information and can be dropped or moved to a system-naming sentence.

**Suggested revision**

> We propose an anomaly-detection front end that filters degenerate generations before they reach Mechanical Turk annotators for Likert evaluation. Our working hypothesis — which we test in §X rather than assume — is that once the share of incoherent items in a HIT exceeds a threshold, contrast effects degrade rating quality for the remaining items [cite anchoring work]. The gate is designed to be cheap, calibrated, and architecture-agnostic, so that it can be dropped between any open-ended generator and any AMT pipeline without retraining either.
>
> We adopt a deep one-class formulation. Prior work reports that deep anomaly detectors are competitive with or stronger than classical baselines (isolation forest, LOF, one-class SVM) on high-dimensional text embeddings [cite]; we therefore use Deep SVDD [Ruff et al., 2018] and defer a head-to-head comparison to §Y.
>
> Candidates are encoded by a frozen MPNet sentence encoder [Song et al., 2020]. A Deep SVDD head is trained on the embeddings of ~12k human-written stories from the WritingPrompts corpus [Fan et al., 2018]; training converges within 30 epochs on a single GPU. At inference, each candidate is scored by squared Euclidean distance from the learned center, and candidates above the 90th percentile of the human score distribution are filtered (an explicit ~10% false-positive rate on in-distribution stories, chosen to bound annotator exposure to degenerate items; see §Z for sensitivity).
>
> Holding the encoder fixed isolates the contribution of the scoring head to any downstream change in AMT ratings. Whether the gate generalizes across generator architectures and target genres is an empirical question we address in §W.

Uncertainty: The specific ADBench / Han et al. and Mathur et al. citations are recalled from memory and should be verified before insertion; the WritingPrompts (Fan et al., 2018) and MPNet (Song et al., 2020) references are standard. The claim that classical detectors remain competitive in some regimes is well established in the anomaly-detection benchmarking literature, but the exact strength of that claim for sentence-embedding inputs should be checked against a current survey.
